author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)  Abydos entrance Welcome to the latest installment in the KERI Tutorial Series! In a prior article we learned how to use KERI to sign and verify simple messages. This tutorial goes to the full depth of issuing and verifying credentials (ACDCs). We outfit our team of explorers bound on a treasure hunting journey with high tech security tags so the Gatekeeper will recognize them and permit them entrance into the most rewarding, yet dangerous, caverns of the secret underground chambers beneath the Osireion at Abydos. Update (warning): KERIpy agent now removed. Post pending update. o use the new agent which is KERIA as well as the new IPEX protocol for credential handling (Issuance and Presentation Exchange).

content: 09/27/2023: As of KERIpy pull request 577 the old, deprecated KERIpy agent (Mark I agent) used in this blog post is now removed. This post will be updated to use the new agent which is KERIA as well as the new IPEX protocol for credential handling (Issuance and Presentation Exchange).

author: Kent Bull
content: Egyptian Story Time
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: An ancient city in Egypt from the time of the Pharaohs Abydos has long been known as a burial and memorial site for the ancient rulers. Yet, until recently, what had been kept a secret is the true purpose of Abydos and the Osireion, an entrance to an underground world full of bounty and treasure where the Egyptian rulers stored their most precious things.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Even with this revelation few could master the secret of how to travel between the Osireion and this underground world. Only two ever reliably figured it out, first the wise Ramiel and then his strong student Zaqiel. After they retrieved all the riches and treasure they wanted they realized they had an opportunity on their hands as guides for future explorers. Thus ATHENA was born.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Opportunity
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To establish a new standard in treasure hunting trust, and to capitalize on their opportunity, Ramiel and Zaqiel formed the Abydos Treasure Hunting Expedition Navigators Assembly (ATHENA) and began looking for a technology partner to build their Journey Mark trust system. Ramiel took on the role of the Wise Man to evaluate, grant marks to, and guide treasure hunters to Abydos while Zaqiel, the stronger of the two, took on the role of the Osireion Gatekeeper to ensure only those accepted by Ramiel would be given the opportunity to learn to travel to the underground world.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: What ATHENA seeks to create
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: According to ATHENA, explorers desiring entrance through the Osireion must present a verifiable, secure proof signed by the Wise Man in order to be accepted by the Gatekeeper. Potential explorers must signify their intent to embark on a journey by presenting the Wise Man with another secure proof.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

content: The diagram below shows the end state of their desired network.

author: Kent Bull
content: On a practical note, get some refreshments and settle in as this post is large and contains many parts. It may take a few sessions to fully absorb the material depending on your existing familiarity with KERI and ACDC and the related Github repositories.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: You meet the two
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: So how did you ever come to meet up with Ramiel and Zaqiel at ATHENA? It turns out the story is more believable than you might think.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: tories. You meet the two So how did you ever come to meet up with Ramiel and Zaqiel at ATHENA? It turns out the story is more believable than you might think.  Image While on a casual stroll at the Internet Identity Workshop (IIW) you had the good fortune of bumping into Ramiel and Zaqiel and they asked you how you would build the ATHENA security system. Since you had just left Dr. Samuel Smith’s session on composable event streaming representation (CESR) and had earlier been to Drummond Reed’s session KERI for Muggles where you learned about key event receipt infrastructure (KERI) and authentic chained data containers (ACDC), you realized they were the best decentralized key management infrastructure (DKMI) available and figured this would be the right tool to get the job done for ATHENA. Image After hearing the details from Ramiel you became excited about the opportunity. You enthusiastically shook hands with them and began your work. Then, feeling a little in over your head and hoping for a concise and useful guide on KERI and ACDC to get you started on your development path, you hear Kaliya Identity Woman Young casually mention a link in the session notes she hounded the session presenter to write down. Grateful for her efforts you began to feel a sense of relief as you turned to the IIW QiQo site and found this blog post. A rush of hope and encouragement came and you dug in realizing you found the KERI manual you needed to get a simple prototype up and running.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

Once you are up and running with the contents of this blog post you will have a foundation upon which to explore the other topics listed above. For now, let’s go to Abydos! Going to the Osireion at Abydos  Image Our Task Create the network foundation.
Create digital identities for each involved party.
Give a verifiable journey description to each interested explorer.
Request a Journey Mark for each explorer from the Wise Man.
As the Wise Man issue the Journey Mark to each member of the explorer party.
Present the Journey Marks to the Gatekeeper and grant access to the Osireion at Abydos. Environment Setup Check your environment-specific platform installation instructions for additional clarity though use the “All Platforms” list for a list of dependencies that ultimately must be installed on your machine and available from the command line.

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: If you want to quickly start up a network rather than working through the entire blog post piece by piece then you can use the workflow.sh script to start the whole network and perform all of the actions including schema linking, configuration file writing, witness network startup, controller configuration and startup, OOBI configuration, credential issuance, and credential revocation.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Jump to the end with the workflow.sh script

author: Kent Bull
content: workflow.sh
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The workflow script accepts a few different ways of running it:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Jump to the end with the workflow.sh script

author: Kent Bull
content:
# from within the abydos-tutorial directory
#
# Option 1: run with the KLI and no clearing
./workflow.sh
# this will run the entire workflow yet will not clean up the ~/.keri, 
# ~/.sally, or the /usr/local/var/keri directories so you will need
# to manually clear those out in order to run the workflow script again.
#
# Option 2: run with the KLI and the clearing option
./workflow.sh -c true
# this runs the entire workflow using the KLI and clears out the KERI 
# directories mentioned above
#
# Option 3: run with the KERI Agent (Mark I - deprecated) and the
#           clearing option
./workflow.sh -a true -c true
# this runs the workflow using the KLI only where absolutely necessary
# and then cURL requests to the Agent API to perform the majority of the 
# workflow

BASH

content: This is a great way to get you up and running. The entire workflow takes only 3 minutes for the KLI flow and 2.5 minutes for the Agent workflow. Reading through the BASH script may be the fastest way for you to learn how to use KERI if you are ready for that level of detail.

author: Kent Bull

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: four agents, one for each controller (Mark I Agent – deprecated)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: a credential caching server
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

content: four distinct credential types.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: a webhook
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: You will, of course, set up all of the configuration files and keystores for each witness and controller as well as perform all of the OOBI configuration for the network to function. See below for an overview.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Network Overview
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta:
m the majority of the 
# workflow

BASH This is a great way to get you up and running. The entire workflow takes only 3 minutes for the KLI flow and 2.5 minutes for the Agent workflow. Reading through the BASH script may be the fastest way for you to learn how to use KERI if you are ready for that level of detail. ATHENA Network The ATHENA network includes: three witnesses
four controllers (with one custom controller, the Abydos Gatekeeper)
four agents, one for each controller (Mark I Agent – deprecated)
a credential caching server
four distinct credential types.
a webhook You will, of course, set up all of the configuration files and keystores for each witness and controller as well as perform all of the OOBI configuration for the network to function. See below for an overview. Network Overview
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Controllers
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: There is one controller each for Richard, Elayne, Zaqiel, and Ramiel. You will write the configuration files for each controller and create and incept their keystores.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Custom Controller
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The one custom controller is the one for Zaqiel, the Abydos Gatekeeper controller. This controller needs to be customized in order to make the access control decisions for Abydos. This includes custom credential validators and presentation response payload constructors.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Witnesses
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The same three witnesses are used for all of the controllers. Using the same witnesses for everything isn’t required and would be bad practice, though it simplifies the architecture for this post. You could easily use separate witnesses per controller with just a bit more tedious work. You will write the witness configuration files for each witness and set them up using the KLI.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credential Caching Server
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The vLEI-server from the vLEI repository is used as the credential caching server. It is a very simple HTTP fileserver that serves up ACDC schemas upon HTTP request. It would easily be implementable in any other language or tech stack if you needed it to be.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Agents
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The agents use the current agent code in the KERIpy repository, called the Mark I Agent. This agent code is now deprecated even though it is nearly fully fleshed out. The new Mark II Agent is being developed as a separate repository in the KERIA repository.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Webhook
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The webhook is a simple HTTP server built on the ioflo/hio library executed with sally hook demo. All it does is receive HTTP requests from the Abydos Gatekeeper custom controller, parse the JSON body, and print all of the headers and body values.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: sally hook demo
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Foundational Vocabulary and Theory
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Next we cover some basic vocabulary and acronyms so you can make your way through this post. I recommend you review Nuttawut Kongsuwan’s KERI jargon summary of the KERI whitepaper for an overview of the terminology in the space. You can always go to the KERI Whitepaper or my KERI Mind Map blog post as well for a list of documents central to the KERI space that will assist you in gaining a full understanding of the concepts and the terminology.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: KERI – Key Event Receipt Infrastructure. A decentralized public key infrastructure for self certifying identifiers aiming at security as a top priority while following the design principle of “minimally sufficient means.” The foundation for the verifiable credentials technology called authentic chained data containers.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: AID -autonomic identifier. a self-certifying identifier that is the primary root of trust in KERI. AIDs are decentralized identifiers that do not rely on a blockchain.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Controller – a node or an individual set of cryptographic keys, their key event logs (KELs), and associated data. Consists of a keystore directory, a set of autonomic identifiers (AIDs), key event logs, any credentials, transaction event logs, and out of band introductions.Also considered to be a running instance of a KERI controller software such as KERIpy (Python KERI controller) or KERIox (Rust KERI controller).
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Witness – a trusted entity designated by another controller as a witness to anything that happens for an AID controlled by that controller. Each witness must provide witness receipts to each event in a key event log.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: KERIpy – the Python implementation of KERI including the controller implementation. This Python implementation is considered authoritative as the KERI core team builds it along with Dr. Samuel M. Smith.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Agent – The HTTP API to a KERI controller.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: OOBI – out of band introduction. The core discovery mechanism of KERI. Each OOBI defines a path of communication between controllers or a location where a resource like an ACDC schema resides. Think of this as something like a DNS entry or an iptables entry, or an address in an address book. If you have an OOBI for a controller or object you want to talk to then you can reach it over the internet.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ACDC – authentic chained data container; verifiable credential. The fundamental unit of data shared between KERI controllers meant for instrumenting business process workflows that use verifiable credentials.An append-only, graph-linked data structure that is secure and verifiable.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Edge – a link (or chain) between two ACDCs.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ACDC Schema – the detailed description of precisely what data is allowed to be placed into any given ACDC.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credential Registry – where ACDCs are issued from.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Ricardian Contract – In ACDCs they are a way to link the legal system to specific ACDCs. This comes in the Rules section of ACDCs.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Admittance Workflow
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The purpose of all of this business about identifiers and credentials is to support a basic admittance workflow into Abydos, specifically the Osireion at the back of Abydos. In order to support this workflow we use verifiable credentials.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credentials Overview
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: For the explorers to be granted admittance into Abydos the admittance workflow must be followed:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The Wise Man must issue TreasureHuntingJourney credentials to each explorer wishing to go on a treasure hunt to Abydos.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Each explorer must issue a JourneyMarkRequest credential for the treasure hunt they want to embark on by pointing to a specific TreasureHuntingJourney credential in their request.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As the Wise Man approves each explorer he is to issue a JourneyMark as an approval of the explorer’s desire to embark on the journey. This approval should point to the specific request each explorer made for a specific journey.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once a sufficient number of JourneyMark credentials are issued by the Wise Man and the journey threshold has been crossed he is to issue a JourneyCharter to each explorer signifying his approval of their journey.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The JourneyCharter credential is to be presented by each explorer to the Gatekeeper in order to be granted entrance into Abydos.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To understand this workflow we first cover the credential map itself, how to write a credential, and how to link each of the schemas together using the KASLCred tool.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Visual workflows and use cases for credential issuance
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Each of the credential issuance flows is described visually below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Visual workflows and use cases for credential issuance

author: Kent Bull
content: 1 – TreasureHuntingJourney issuance flow
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As a Wise Man (ATHENA official) I want to provide confidence to potential explorers so that I may attract them to go on a treasure hunting journey.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To do this the WiseMan issues the TreasureHuntingJourney credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta:
the journey threshold has been crossed he is to issue a JourneyCharter to each explorer signifying his approval of their journey.
The JourneyCharter credential is to be presented by each explorer to the Gatekeeper in order to be granted entrance into Abydos. To understand this workflow we first cover the credential map itself, how to write a credential, and how to link each of the schemas together using the KASLCred tool. Visual workflows and use cases for credential issuance Each of the credential issuance flows is described visually below. 1 – TreasureHuntingJourney issuance flow As a Wise Man (ATHENA official) I want to provide confidence to potential explorers so that I may attract them to go on a treasure hunting journey. To do this the WiseMan issues the TreasureHuntingJourney credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: 2 – JourneyMarkRequest issuance flow
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As an Explorer I want to indicate a serious interest on a specific TreasureHuntingJourney so that ATHENA will hold my spot.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To do this an Explorer (or Librarian) issues to the WiseMan a JourneyMarkRequest pointing to a specific TreasureHuntingJourney credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: over the credential map itself, how to write a credential, and how to link each of the schemas together using the KASLCred tool. Visual workflows and use cases for credential issuance Each of the credential issuance flows is described visually below. 1 – TreasureHuntingJourney issuance flow As a Wise Man (ATHENA official) I want to provide confidence to potential explorers so that I may attract them to go on a treasure hunting journey. To do this the WiseMan issues the TreasureHuntingJourney credential.  2 – JourneyMarkRequest issuance flow As an Explorer I want to indicate a serious interest on a specific TreasureHuntingJourney so that ATHENA will hold my spot. To do this an Explorer (or Librarian) issues to the WiseMan a JourneyMarkRequest pointing to a specific TreasureHuntingJourney credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: 3 – JourneyMark issuance flow
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As a Wise Man I want to endorse a particular Explorer for a specific TreasureHuntingJourney so that the journey can be chartered once a threshold of party membership is reached.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To do this the Wise Man issues the JourneyMark to those Explorers whose JourneyMarkRequests he approves. The JourneyMark points to the specific JourneyMarkRequest submitted by an explorer.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

To do this the WiseMan issues the TreasureHuntingJourney credential.  2 – JourneyMarkRequest issuance flow As an Explorer I want to indicate a serious interest on a specific TreasureHuntingJourney so that ATHENA will hold my spot. To do this an Explorer (or Librarian) issues to the WiseMan a JourneyMarkRequest pointing to a specific TreasureHuntingJourney credential.  3 – JourneyMark issuance flow As a Wise Man I want to endorse a particular Explorer for a specific TreasureHuntingJourney so that the journey can be chartered once a threshold of party membership is reached. To do this the Wise Man issues the JourneyMark to those Explorers whose JourneyMarkRequests he approves. The JourneyMark points to the specific JourneyMarkRequest submitted by an explorer.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: 4 – JourneyCharter issuance flow
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As a Wise Man I want to charter a treasure hunting journey once a party membership threshold is reached so that the explorers can present this charter credential to the Gatekeeper and gain entrance into Abydos.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To do this the WiseMan issues the JourneyCharter credential to each Explorer going on the journey indicated in the TreasureHuntingJourney credential. The JourneyCharter points to both the JourneyMark and the TreasureHuntingJourney credential.

To do this the WiseMan issues the JourneyCharter credential to each Explorer going on the journey indicated in the TreasureHuntingJourney credential. author: Kent Bull

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta:
ATHENA Credential Graph  ATHENA credential graph eHuntingJourney: One for the Wise Man to describe the overall treasure hunting experience from start to end, treasure splits for hunting party members, the destination, and the like. This will be issued by the Wise Man to each potential explorer.
(2) JourneyMarkRequest: One for a potential explorer to request to join the treasure hunting journey. This will be issued to the Wise Man by an explorer.
(3) JourneyMark: One for the Wise man to issue to the accepted explorers so the Gatekeeper can know who the Wise Man has authorized for the journey.
(4) JourneyCharter: One that the explorer party can use to prove they are authorized for their journey to all who care to know as well as for the Gatekeeper to verify that they were authorized as a party.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In order to understand the credential schemas we must first cover some background knowledge on why schemas in ACDC are constructed the way they are, the purpose they serve, and how to use them.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ACDC Schema Writing
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: JSON Schema is the schema language ACDC schemas are expressed in. Beyond this schema language self addressing data and self addressing identifiers are the mechanism used to verify schemas. With security first as a design principle self addressing data combines well with JSON schema to provide a secure schema capability for the KERI and ACDC ecosystem.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ACDC Credential Schemas with JSON Schema
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: For each ACDC we use there must be a schema defining the shape of the data in the container or credential. This schema must be verifiable and protected from schema revocation and schema malleability attacks. Using self addressing data (SAD) through the use of self addressing identifiers (SAIDs) makes it easy to accomplish both of these security objectives along with the business process objective of having a verifiable shape or envelope for our data.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

RICHARD_CRED_SAID=$(kli vc list --name explorer --alias richard --said --schema EIxAox3KEhiQ_yCwXWeriQ3ruPWbgK94NDDkHAZCuP9l)

author: Kent Bull
content: As you can see it has metadata, properties, attributes, and rules yet no edges. This is because this TreasureHuntingJourney ACDC does not extend nor connect with any other ACDC. This ACDC could appear as an edge in another ACDC though it does not have any edges itself.

author: Kent Bull
content: Keep in mind that this instance of the TreasureHuntingJourney schema does not have any “$id” properties filled out yet. This tells you we are still writing the schema and are working from the template that will have the SAIDs added to, or “SAIDified.” We will do that step later after we finish writing the schema itself.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: First we shall explain the attributes of the schema from outer to inner, top to bottom. Each of the attributes has a specific purpose we get into below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: SCHEMA description – top-layer
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: While ACDC schemas use the JSON Schema specification in most regards there is one exception where ACDC schemas repurpose the “id”field.Thetop−mostid, $schema, title, description, type, credentialType, and version properties are about the ACDC schema itself. What is contained inside of the ACDC is described by what comes in the properties section which is further clarified by the additionalProperties and required attributes.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: $id : The self addressing identifier (SAID) of the overall schema. This can only be computed when all of the dependent SAIDs for all attribute (e), edge (e), and rule (r) blocks have been computed and embedded within the respective $id attributes of each block. This field is repurposed to provide a unique identifier of the schema instance. This is because in ACDCs all schemas are self addressing and so there is no separate document to retrieve such as in non-ACDC credentials, such as JSON-LD credentials, where the idfieldcontainsabaseURIfragmentthatneedstoberesolvedtoaschema.SincetheschemaiskeyedandstoredbySAIDratherthanaRetrievalURIthenthereislittletonobenefitfromstoringaretrievalURIintheid section.See the $id section of the ACDC specification for a more complete explanation. Example Value: EDiuqE2fD2MhrSpZhAzlZn8XLSJiHt1pyy1eEWB-l1qq
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull

This can only be computed when all of the dependent SAIDs for all attribute (e), edge (e), and rule (r) blocks have been computed and embedded within the respective $id attributes of each block. This field is repurposed to provide a unique identifier of the schema instance. author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The TreasureHuntingJourney credential is intended to provide information to prospective explorers about a journey they could embark on. This includes where the journey is headed, the destination, the proportion of treasure proceeds split amongst each journey party member, the minimum party threshold for the journey to be chartered, as well as the individual who chartered the journey.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The properties section of an ACDC schema is the location to include custom business-process data as shown above for the TreasureHuntingJourney credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Writing The Properties schema
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Writing a schema to describe this small set of data requires a rather verbose description using JSON schema and though tedious is simple to understand once you get the hang of it.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In addition to this data there is a rules section “r” we will get into later when we talk about Ricardian Contracts though for now we focus only on the data going into the credential, the “a” section.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Writing a properties section with JSON Schema to describe this requires both metadata and an attributes section.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

Writing The Properties schema Writing a schema to describe this small set of data requires a rather verbose description using JSON schema and though tedious is simple to understand once you get the hang of it. In addition to this data there is a rules section “r” we will get into later when we talk about Ricardian Contracts though for now we focus only on the data going into the credential, the “a” section. Writing a properties section with JSON Schema to describe this requires both metadata and an attributes section.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The ACDC functionality of KERI requires the presence of a number of metadata attributes in the properties section of a JSON Schema document as shown below. Each attribute is linked to the appropriate section in the ACDC specification. The overall field map description is provided here: Top-Level Fields.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: v – “Version”: This describes the version of the serialization format and size used to write the schema.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: d – “SAID or identifier”: This provides a stable, universal, cryptographically verifiable and agile reference to the properties block.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: u – “Universally Unique Identifier as a Nonce”: A salty nonce used to provide cryptographic security to the “d” field to protect against brute force attacks such as rainbow table attacks to guess the block’s contents. This field is optional.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: i – “Autonomic Identifier”: The identifier of the issuer of this ACDC.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ri – “Registry Identifier (Derived Identifier)”: The registry is the credential registry that this particular ACDC was issued from. This is derived from the “i” field for the issuer who issued the ACDC. A controller can create and use one or more registries.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: s – “Schema SAID”: The SAID of the schema to be used to validate this properties block.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: a – “Attribute map”: The list of attributes that defines this schema. This is where the JSON data from above gets placed into the credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: r – “Rules section”: This is where any Ricardian Contracts (code as law) are stored in the credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Other than the metadata attributes the “a” and “r” properties, attributes and rules, are the more interesting parts. The “a” section is where you will describe the data properties that will be placed in your ACDC.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: TreasureHuntingJourney Attributes Section
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The attributes section can be one of two things as shown in the JSON schema, either a SAID or an inner block with its own properties.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
...
"a":

      "description": "Attributes block SAID",
      "type": "string"

      "type": "object",
      "properties": { 
...

author: Kent Bull
content: oneOf Operator and compact ACDC
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The oneOf operator in JSON Schema means that the “a” section can be one of either of these two things. For ACDCs this means that the “a” block can be either the condensed version, which is the SAID of the “a” block, or it can be the entire contents of the “a” block. The condensed version is called a “Compact ACDC” and can be seen as equivalent to the un-condensed, or un-compacted version of the ACDC.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: We are not covering compact ACDCs in this article so we move on to the full or un-compacted ACDC.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Full or un-compacted ACDC

author: Kent Bull
content: The inner properties block has a set of metadata fields that the ACDC code in KERI fills out for you including the “d” (SAID), “i” (issuee), and “dt” (date and time stamp) fields. They are required attributes in the top level of the inner properties block and look like the following in the JSON schemas where they appear:

author: Kent Bull
content: The “$id” field is a SAID of the “a” attributes block. This provides the “a” block with cryptographic protection and verifiability when signed by a KERI key.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

content: Inner Properties Block Custom Fields
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Linking Schemas
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: One concept we have not yet covered is how to link schemas together. We cover this next in the Schema Linking and Verification section via the use of the KASLCred utility.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Schema Linking and Verification
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Linking schemas with self addressing data is part of the way ACDCs provide security, specifically end-verifiability, of credentials. For data to be self addressing it must contain an identifier that is computed from the contents of the data, a digest. Self addressing data protects against security exploits like schema malleability and schema revocation attacks while also being an easy way to verify the contents of the data. These self addressing identifiers are placed in the “$id” attributes of the various blocks in ACDC schemas.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Here is an example self addressing identifier for the TreasureHuntingJourney ACDC shown above: EEXZuecxP4Y3xZxvA_DtnrPX8nbSDPeGaMIxNKvLVENb
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: EEXZuecxP4Y3xZxvA_DtnrPX8nbSDPeGaMIxNKvLVENb
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Self Addressing Data (SAD) and Self Addressing Identifiers (SAIDs)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The self addressing identifier (SAID) is like a key in a key-value pair and the data is like the value except that the identifier computation algorithm allows for the SAID to be embedded in the data. This requires a two pass process, one to calculate the identifier, and the second to embed it within the data. Verifying self addressing data also requires a two-pass verification function, one pass to remove the embedded identifier, and the second to verify the data against the SAID. To fully understand the self addressing identifier concept refer to the IETF Draft Specification for Self-Addressing IDentifier (SAID) by Samuel smith.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Self Addressing Data (SAD) and Self Addressing Identifiers (SAIDs)

author: Kent Bull
content: Schema Evaluation to compute SAIDs
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Using self addressing identifiers (SAIDs) with a graph of schemas means that the credential schemas must be evaluated in a deterministic order and have their “$id” properties populated from the inner-most or top-most schema to the outer-most or bottom-most schema. In this sense the outer-most schema points to the innermost schema and the bottom-most schema points to the top-most schema as in the ATHENA Credential Graph diagram above and reproduced below for reference. This proper order requires computing a dependency graph of the schemas based on their edges (dependencies) in order to facilitate generating identifiers on the inner-most (or top-most) schemas and then each layer outward to the outermost (or bottom-most) schemas.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: on to compute SAIDs Using self addressing identifiers (SAIDs) with a graph of schemas means that the credential schemas must be evaluated in a deterministic order and have their “$id” properties populated from the inner-most or top-most schema to the outer-most or bottom-most schema. In this sense the outer-most schema points to the innermost schema and the bottom-most schema points to the top-most schema as in the ATHENA Credential Graph diagram above and reproduced below for reference. This proper order requires computing a dependency graph of the schemas based on their edges (dependencies) in order to facilitate generating identifiers on the inner-most (or top-most) schemas and then each layer outward to the outermost (or bottom-most) schemas.  athena credential graph Graph Evaluation Order In our case our graph must be generated in the following order:

author: Kent Bull
content: Your options here are to either do this by hand with the kli saidify command and manually editing all of the schema files or you can use the code in the generate.py file from the WebOfTrust/vLEI repository as a guide. Alternatively you could use the KASLCred Python package to do all of the linking for you. For this post we will use KASLCred in the interest of time.

author: Kent Bull
content: While the intricacies of parsing ACDC schemas and adding SAIDs in the “$id” attributes are interesting, also called “saidifying” the schemas, in the interest of time we will use the KASLCred utility written to do all the saidifying for you. You can go read the Python code of the TetraVeda/kaslcred repository if you want to see how it was done. It’s only a few hundred lines of code.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Evaluation Order
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As you can see this list of schemas is out of order. The first item in the list, JourneyCharter, should be evaluated last. And the third item in the list, TreasureHuntingJourney, should be evaluated first. This is no problem for KASLCred as it reads the dependencies array and reorders the schemas to be evaluated in the order where the least dependent item (with no dependencies) is evaluated first and then the next most dependent item is evaluated after all of its dependencies is evaluated and so forth for all dependencies.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Using KASLCred is as simple as a Pip Install and a python -m invocation once you have Libsodium and KERI installed:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: python -m
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
# Install KERI and Libsodium as noted in the installation instructions above
pip install kaslcred==0.0.8
# Set ABYDOS_REPO_DIR to where you cloned the abydos-tutorial repo.
# Usage: python -m kaslcred [schemas dir] [results dir] [schema map file]
python -m kaslcred \
  ${ABYDOS_REPO_DIR}/athena/schemas \
  ${ABYDOS_REPO_DIR}/athena/saidified_schemas \
  ${ABYDOS_REPO_DIR}/athena/schemas/athena-schema-map.json

BASH

author: Kent Bull
content: Schema caching is first item to be set up as all of the controllers depend on it. You could set up witnesses first as well. It is up to you.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Schema Caching
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In order to successfully receive, validate, and handle an ACDC each KERI controller using that ACDC must be able to resolve and load the schema for that ACDC. The schemas are resolved based on the SAID of the schema using an out of band introduction (OOBI) that indicates the location the schema is stored at and can be retrieved from. The controller may either be configured with the location of this schema on startup in the controller bootstrap JSON configuration file or be configured at runtime through usage of the OOBI resolution mechanism.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: vLEI-server
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: he schemas are resolved based on the SAID of the schema using an out of band introduction (OOBI) that indicates the location the schema is stored at and can be retrieved from. The controller may either be configured with the location of this schema on startup in the controller bootstrap JSON configuration file or be configured at runtime through usage of the OOBI resolution mechanism. vLEI-server  vLEI-server The vLEI-server provides a convenient implementation of a simple caching server that meets our needs. All the vLEI caching server (vLEI-server) does is make the schemas available as JSON objects as responses to HTTP requests. It could very easily be rewritten in your favorite language if you wanted a fun, easy challenge. (Hint, write it in Rust, the KERI community will be happy with you.) Once you have the vLEI repository cloned and the vLEI-server installed as described in the installation instructions above you can run the vLEI-server with a command similar to the following:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The vLEI-server provides a convenient implementation of a simple caching server that meets our needs. All the vLEI caching server (vLEI-server) does is make the schemas available as JSON objects as responses to HTTP requests. It could very easily be rewritten in your favorite language if you wanted a fun, easy challenge. (Hint, write it in Rust, the KERI community will be happy with you.)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once you have the vLEI repository cloned and the vLEI-server installed as described in the installation instructions above you can run the vLEI-server with a command similar to the following:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
# Change directories to the abydos-tutorial repository directory.
# Set ATHENA_DIR to be your abydos-tutorial/athena directory.
vLEI-server \
  -s "${ATHENA_DIR}"/saidified_schemas \
  -c "${ATHENA_DIR}"/cache/acdc \
  -o "${ATHENA_DIR}"/cache/oobis

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Our witness network consists of three of the witnesses used in the KERI Demonstration Witness network meaning we use the same cryptographic key salts, keystore names, and alias names.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: This means we need a configuration file for each of the witnesses. The document symbols you see on the preceding diagram show the filenames for each of the witnesses represented by a gray circle.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

Location and Directory Structure JSON configuration files for KERI witnesses must be placed inside a particular directory structure. This directory structure is as followsCONFIG_ROOT/keri/cf/main
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The reason for this is that the KERI Configer module uses the root path of “keri/cf” and the sub path of “main” as see in the Configer.init function. You can configure the CONFIG_ROOT directory path by customizing the --config-dir property though the keri/cf/main directory path will be created for you inside of the CONFIG_ROOT directory if they do not already exist.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --config-dir
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: There must be a key in the configuration object named the same as the value passed into the --alias [alias] property for the subsequent kli witness start command. This applies to KERIpy, the Python implementation of KERI and the reference implementation of the KERI and ACDC specification.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --alias [alias]
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli witness start
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Refer specifically to KERIpy habbing.pyL971 in the reconfigure function to see how the configuration file must have an appropriately named key. Keep in mind that this function documentation states that “Config file is meant to be read only at init not changed by app at run time” so you should make any changes to this configuration before the kli witness start commands are run since any changes during run time will not be recognized until the witness is stopped and then started again.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: reconfigure
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The values inside the configuration file are as follows. Sub-properties such as the “dt” property inside of “wan” are indicated with the dot notation.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “wan”: The named configuration block property for a given witness. “wan.dt”: The date-time stamp when the configuration was created.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “wan.curls”: Controller URLs. This is the list of URLs that indicate the protocol and ports for the witness to listen on. You can see both TCP port 5632 and HTTP port 5642 in the above example.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Other properties that are not shown above yet can be presented include:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “durls”: Data URLs. Another section for OOBI URLs to process. Typically you put credential OOBIs in this section when you want the witness to resolve them on startup. You could put these URLs in the “iurls” section as well.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “wurls”: Well-known OOBIs to resolve on startup. TODO clarify difference between and purpose of each type of URL here.

We will end up with the following three keystores:

author: Kent Bull
content: We use the kli init command to create each of the keystores.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli init
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A witness requires a keystore to be created first in order to start the witness later. Do this with a command like the following:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli init --name wan --salt 0AB3YW5uLXRoZS13aXRuZXNz --nopasscode \
    --config-dir "${CONFIG_DIR}" \
    --config-file main/wan-witness

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli init --name wan --salt 0AB3YW5uLXRoZS13aXRuZXNz --nopasscode \
    --config-dir "${CONFIG_DIR}" \
    --config-file main/wan-witness
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: You can see you must name the witness and pass in a configuration directory and file. The configuration file must have the “main/” path prefix on it since the underlying Configer class defaults the “base” path property to be “main” as seen on Configer:L50 here. You don’t need to add the “.json” suffix since KERIpy will do that for you. The filename alone is sufficient.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull

Place each of the JSON configuration files in your configuration directory with the appropriate directory suffix like so:$CONFIG_DIR/keri/cf/main

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Cryptographic Salts (seeds)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The salt used here is the same as both what is used in the Abydos Tutorial witness set as well as what is used for the demo command kli witness demo that starts six witnesses for you. This is insecure. For production you should use separate salts. Using the same salt means the keys generated from the salt will be the same. So you may conclude that protecting your salt is as important as protecting your keys themselves similar to protecting your Bitcoin or Ethereum seed phrase and private keys.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli witness demo
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Whatever you do make sure your seeds do not get in the hands of people who shouldn’t have them and be sure to use highly secure deployment mechanisms to inject seeds into your witness, agent, and controller deployments.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Moving on, next we start the witness.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Witness Start Command: kli witness start
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Starting a witness is very simple yet it does depend on code recently added to KERIpy to support the --config-dir and --config-file arguments so make sure you install the latest version of KERIpy from source with ***python -m pip install -e ./*** from the KERIpy repository root directory. This will give you at least KERIpy 1.0.0 release locally whether or not the release has been pushed to PyPi or GitHub yet.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Witness Start Command: kli witness start

author: Kent Bull
content: --config-dir
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --config-file
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ***python -m pip install -e ./***
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli witness start --name wan --alias wan \
  -T ${WAN_WITNESS_TCP_PORT} \
  -H ${WAN_WITNESS_HTTP_PORT} \
  --config-dir "${CONFIG_DIR}" \
  --config-file wan-witness

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull

kli witness start --name wan --alias wan \
  -T ${WAN_WITNESS_TCP_PORT} \
  -H ${WAN_WITNESS_HTTP_PORT} \
  --config-dir "${CONFIG_DIR}" \
  --config-file wan-witness
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Ports and the alias name are a few additional configuration options here. Remember that the “alias” property must match the name used in the witness configuration file you wrote earlier in which you defined the TCP and HTTP endpoints for your witness to listen on. The other arguments are as they seem, -T for the TCP port and -H for the HTTP port the witness will listen on.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once you have all of the witnesses set up and started your next task is to set up the KERI controllers for Richard, Elayne, Ramiel, and Zaqiel. This involves writing a bootstrap configuration file which we get into next.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Witness Start Command: kli witness start

author: Kent Bull
content: KERI Controller Setup
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Creating and setting up controllers can happen in one of two ways, with the KERI command line, the KLI, or with the KERI Agent API (called the Mark I Agent, currently deprecated). The difference between the two is that the KLI method does not start a running process or daemon while the Agent runs as a daemon. An important note on the existing Agent API in KERIpy is that Agent API is now deprecated in favor of the new multi-tenant Agent API being written as a separate deployable called KERIA (called the Mark II Agent).
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: While the existing Agent API is deprecated it is nonetheless useful to see how the KLI compares to using REST requests in the Agent API. You get an idea of how you might write your own API on top of a KERI controller or how you might contribute to the agent implementations. Once the Mark II Agent API is ready this article will be updated with the new API.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: There are three general steps to setting up a controller:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Write the controller configuration file.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Initialize the controller’s keystore
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Create the first keypair for the controller, called a prefix, by performing an inception event. This involves using an alias to label that particular prefix.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The KLI flow is shorter than the Agent flow though they both accomplish the same objective. You may follow along in the workflow.sh script with the code for setting up each of the four controllers in the following functions:

author: Kent Bull
content: Four Controllers
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: There are four controllers to be created, one each for Richard, Elayne, Ramiel, and Zaqiel. This involves writing configuration files with OOBIs for witnesses, and credential schemas, initializing controller keystores, performing the inception events, and, in the case of using agents, starting the KERI Agent for each controller. Below is a simplified diagram of the controllers and the out of OOBI connections that must be made between the controllers to support credential issuance and presentation.

author: Kent Bull
content: Controller Bootstrap Configuration File
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A bootstrap configuration file instructs a KERI controller regarding which OOBI URLs it needs to resolve on initial startup. This is one of two ways a KERIpy controller can resolve OOBIs, either at bootstrap time or after a controller has been initialized. The bootstrap configuration file includes both a list of controller OOBIs and a list of credential schema OOBIs. The list of controller OOBIs is usually a list of OOBIs for each witnesses that will be used to perform the inception event though it can be expanded to other controllers beyond the witness pool.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: When first starting a controller it reads in the configuration file specified by the --config-file argument to either the kli init or kli agent start commands. Beware that the configuration file is not re-read on subsequent startups. If you need a controller to resolve more OOBIs after initial bootstrapping then use the kli oobi resolve command or equivalent Agent REST request.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Next we learn how to write each of the two OOBI lists for both the “iurls” and the “durls” sections of the configuration files.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: IURLS: Writing controller OOBIs for the “iurls” section
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: An OOBI URLs uses the following URL scheme:“[protocol]://[host]:[port]/oobi/[prefix]“
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: IURLS: Writing controller OOBIs for the “iurls” section

author: Kent Bull
content: [protocol]://[host]:[port]/oobi/[prefix]
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The protocol is typically “http” though may be “https” or “tcp”. Host and port will be specific to your deployment. Creating a prefix involves many steps as shown in a later section. Without jumping ahead you can use the prefixes automatically created during the kli witness start command you ran earlier. This created a set of keystores which also created a set of prefixes for each witness. You can get the prefixes you created with the kli status command as shown below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: IURLS: Writing controller OOBIs for the “iurls” section

author: Kent Bull
content: kli witness start
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli status
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Prefixes with kli status
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The prefix is the prefix of the first key in a sequence of KERI key creation events (establishment events). You can get the prefix with the kli status command as shown in the read_witness_prefixes_and_configure function in workflow.sh. Here is an example using kli status and AWK to store witness wan’s prefix in the WAN_PREFIX variable:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli status command
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: workflow.sh
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli status
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: WAN_PREFIX
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
WAN_PREFIX=$(kli status --name wan --alias wan | awk '/Identifier:/ {print $2}')
# Then do echo $WAN_PREFIX to see the value of the prefix
echo $WAN_PREFIX

author: Kent Bull
content: GENERATING KERI DIDs
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The AID prefix is the same prefix that is added into the KERI decentralized identifier (DID) generation function when you run kli did generate. To turn the prefix into a did you do as follows:did:[prefix]You simply place the AID prefix after the characters “did:” and you have a KERI decentralized identifier.For example, using the first OOBI below, the corresponding DID would be:did:keri:BBilc4-L3tFUnfM_wJr4S4OJanAv_VmF_dJNN6vkf2Ha
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

JSON Now that you have the controller and agent bootstrap configuration files set up we show how to start up agents with the KLI. KERI Agents KERI Agent Startup – Mark I agent (deprecated) Creating and handling a controller with the KERI Mark I agent involves a four step process:  Image Start the agent with kli agent start
POST to /boot to initialize the keystore
PUT to /boot to unlock the keystore
POST to /ids/${ALIAS} to perform the inception event There are four agents to be set up for the ATHENA network, one each for Richard, Elayne, Ramiel, and Zaqiel as shown below:  KERI Controller Agent Start (Mark I Agent – deprecated) You will notice that the --insecure option is passed to the agent start command. This is because the first draft of a KERI Agent, the Mark I agent, has a partially complete HTTP signature signing scheme that currently does not function and thus must be disabled with the --insecure option. Without disabling this option you will not be able to communicate at all with a Mark I agent. The next version of KERI Agents is KERIA, the Mark II agent, and this has a fully complete HTTP signature signing scheme intended to work with Signify, a TypeScript edge signing library for KERI.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Switching between the KLI and the KERI Agent API (Mark I – deprecated)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: For the remainder of this tutorial we switch back and forth between the KERI command line interface, the KLI, and the KERI Agent HTTP API, the Mark I version in KERIpy, in order to present a broad understanding of both the ways you can interact with the KERI specification and the work that has been completed in the space. This will provide an opportunity to understand both how the command line interaction looks as well as how a REST API addressing a similar purpose feels and looks.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Switching between the KLI and the KERI Agent API (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content: Each task will be completed first with the KLI and then with the KERI Agent API. The KLI is always up to date with the latest and greatest code in KERIpy. The KERI Agent API will likely eventually be removed from KERIpy once the Mark II agent in KERIA is finished. At present the KERI Agent API in the KERIpy repository is now deprecated and is referred to as the Mark I agent.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Switching between the KLI and the KERI Agent API (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content: For our next task of creating keystores we start first with the KERI Command Line Interface (KLI) to initialize the keystores for all of the participants in this journey including the ATHENA officials and the two explorers. After showing how to accomplish this task with the KLI we switch to perform the same task with the Agent API.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Switching between the KLI and the KERI Agent API (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content: Use one or the other for keystores and inception
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Make sure to either use the KLI or the Agent API to perform keystore creation and inception, not both. You only need to create the keystores once whether your use the KLI or the Agent API.

author: Kent Bull
content: If you don’t know which one you want or need for your scenario then start with the KLI.

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The cryptographic salt is one of the arguments to kli init. This salt is what all of the private keys are derived from so it should be protected as if you were protecting your personal identity documents or your bank account. You can get your own salt with the kli salt command:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli init
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Command
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The other arguments are straightforward, nopasscode, config-dir, and config-file. --nopasscode indicates that there is no passcode to access this keystore.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --nopasscode
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The --config-dir property indicates the directory in which to look for configuration files. Inside this configuration directory the file name from the --config-file option is used to find and select the controller bootstrap configuration file that will be used to initialize this keystore. This option does not need to have a file extension if your configuration file already ends with the “.json” extension.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --config-dir
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --config-file
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli init --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} --salt "${EXPLORER_SALT}" \
  --nopasscode \
  --config-dir "${CONFIG_DIR}" \
  --config-file "${CONTROLLER_BOOTSTRAP_FILE}"

BASH

  --config-file "${CONTROLLER_BOOTSTRAP_FILE}"
