author: Kent Bull
content: This command sets up all of the Lightning Memory Mapped Database instances used by the keystore. These are located in the $HOME/.keri directory.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: KERI Controller AID (Prefix) Create and Incept – KLI INCEPT
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Now the keystore is ready to have a specific key pair created and an inception event performed. This will begin the key event log (KEL) for that key pair. Key pairs are referred to with a label called an “alias.” This alias label is intended to be more human-friendly and is used throughout both the KLI and the Agent API to refer to the key pair.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: KERI Controller AID (Prefix) Create and Incept – KLI INCEPT

author: Kent Bull
content: Alias vs Prefix
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Aliases are used to refer to key pairs since prefixes, the internal reference to key pairs, are not human friendly. For example, an alias can be a word or set of words like “richard” and a prefix looks like the following: "EJS0-vv_OPAQCdJLmkd5dT0EW-mOfhn_Cje4yzRjTv8q". As you can see you won’t be remembering many prefixes which is why the alias system is so helpful.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: "EJS0-vv_OPAQCdJLmkd5dT0EW-mOfhn_Cje4yzRjTv8q"
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Steps to Inception
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Performing an inception event, once the keystore is initialized, requires the following two steps:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Write an inception configuration file.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Execute the inception command whether from the command line or the Agent REST API For the KLI you will provide the inception configuration file as an argument.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: For the Agent API you will provide the inception configuration as JSON in the body of the inception HTTP request.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Four inception events need to be performed on each of the four keystores. Each inception event begins a key event log (KEL), as shown below:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

As you can see you won’t be remembering many prefixes which is why the alias system is so helpful. author: Kent Bull
content: Writing a Controller Inception Configuration File
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Each attribute of a controller inception configuration file is important. The initial set of witnesses, keypair counts, key thresholds, transferability, and threshold of accountable duplicity are all defined in this file.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

    "BLskRTInXnMxWaGqcpSyMgo0nYbalW99cGZESrz3zapM",
    "BIKKuvBwpmDVA4Ds-EpL5bt9OqPzWPja2LigFYZN2YfX"
  ],

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Witness configuration file attributes
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Now an explanation of each attribute, which may also be seen in keripy/src/keri/app/habbing.pyL873 and in keripy/src/keri/app/cli/common/incepting.pyL9:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “transferable” – Transferability refers to whether a KERI autonomic identifier (AID) can have its keys rotated. Technically this refers to having what is called “control authority” changed, control over the private keypairs linked to a KERI AID. When true this means the AID is a transferable AID.When false this means the AID is non-transferable.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “wits”: Witnesses – A list of prefixes identifying the witnesses to include signatures for in the inception event in this controller’s key event log.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “toad”: Threshold of accountable duplicity – The number of agreeing witnesses receipts at or above which the controller accepts accountability for signed key events. Accountability is not accepted by the controller for key events with a number of signatures below this threshold.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “icount”: Inception signature count – The number of keys used to sign the inception event.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “ncount”: Number of rotation signatures count – The number of keys to be used on the next key rotation event. Note that not all pre-rotated keys committed to in the inception event as described Reserve Rotation.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “isith”: Signing threshold for the inception event – The number of key event receipts from distinct witnesses the Inception Event must have in order to be authoritative.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: “nsith”: Signing threshold for the next rotation event – The number of key event receipts from distinct witnesses the next Rotation Event must have in order to be authoritative.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Writing your configuration
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: For all except the “wits” section you can copy and paste the configuration file as-is. The “wits” section contains the AID prefixes that will be specific to your witnesses. If you are using the workflow.sh script with precisely the same salts, witness keystore names, and witness AID aliases, then you can use the configuration from above verbatim.

author: Kent Bull
content: Performing Inception – KLI INCEPT
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Incepting an identifier consists of a set of steps that use keys generated by the user to create, sign, get receipts for, and store an inception event in the key event log (KEL) of a controller. The conceptual steps are outlined next.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Creating the number of cryptographic key pairs as indicated in the “icount” configuration property.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Creating the number of pre-rotated cryptographic key pairs as indicated in the “ncount” configuration property.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Creating a valid, properly formatted inception event signed by enough keys to satisfy the “icount” number.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Adding that inception event to the controller’s key event log.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Sending the inception event to each witness in set of witnesses configured in the inception configuration you wrote above. The set of witnesses is defined in the configuration file passed to the --file argument.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --file
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Receiving key event receipts (KERs) of the inception event from each witness.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Adding the key event receipts from the witnesses to the key event receipt log (KERL) for the controller.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: All of these steps are performed for you when you execute the kli incept command. The below example from workflow.sh shows the inception of Richard’s AID:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli incept
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: workflow.sh
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

kli incept --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --file "${CONTROLLER_INCEPTION_CONFIG_FILE}"

  --file "${CONTROLLER_INCEPTION_CONFIG_FILE}"

author: Kent Bull

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: curl Common Headers, arguments, and pipes
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Headers
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The “Accept: /” and “Content-Type: application/json” HTTP headers are common to all of the commands used in the workflow.sh scripts.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: workflow.sh
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Arguments
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The “-s” argument to cURL is the “silent” option and ensures that only the response body of the target HTTP request is sent to the standard output channel.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The backslash “\” escape character is often used in BASH scripts to make commands more readable by enabling splitting of the commands across multiple lines.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Pipes
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: jq: Heavy usage of the jq program, a lightweight and flexible command-line JSON parser, is made throughout this tutorial. Most often the results of a cURL command will be piped to the jq program and then the jq program will select an attribute from the response body using a selector expression like '.["msg"]' in the example below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: tr: Frequently the tr program is used to trim double quotes off of the JSON output returned from the jq program. As seen in the example below tr is used with the -d flag to delete all tokens of the specified set from input. This is needed since often the value of a cURL request needs to be placed in a BASH variable and the extra quotes returned along with the JSON body are problematic to deal with an so it is easier to remove them altogether.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: -d
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Both jq and tr are often used together in a sequence as shown below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
...(more code causing JSON to be sent to standard out)... \
| jq '.["msg"]' | tr -d '"'

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
...(more code causing JSON to be sent to standard out)... \
| jq '.["msg"]' | tr -d '"'
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: KERI Controller Keystore Create – Agent
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: This operation is a POST to the /boot endpoint which creates a keystore where the body is a JSON object with the value placed in the “name” property of the JSON body used to name the keystore. The “salt” value is the cryptographic salt used as the cryptographic seed to derive all key pairs in the keystore. You can use the kli salt command to generate a new salt.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: /boot
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli salt
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The ${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL} is define on Line 127 of workflow.sh and looks like this:http://127.0.0.1:5620
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
curl -s -X POST "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/boot" \
  -H 'accept: */*' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data "{\"name\": \"${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE}\",
           \"salt\": \"${EXPLORER_SALT}\"}" \
  | jq '.["msg"]' | tr -d '"'

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
curl -s -X POST "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/boot" \
  -H 'accept: */*' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data "{\"name\": \"${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE}\",
           \"salt\": \"${EXPLORER_SALT}\"}" \
  | jq '.["msg"]' | tr -d '"'
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As you can see the “name” and “salt” options are similar to the similarly named arguments to KLI init. The difference here is that they are in the body of the POST request as JSON. The result is piped to “jq” and then “tr” to extract and print the result message to the terminal.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Next the keystore must be unlocked.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: KERI Controller Keystore Unlock – Agent
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Unlocking a keystore is a PUT to the /boot endpoint using the same JSON body as was POSTed to initialize the keystore. This is used to permit a particular Agent to work with a specified keystore. This is useful because an agent may have been restarted after initializing the keystore and so the Agent must establish an authenticated connection to the keystore.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: /boot
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Bear in mind that the salt is being sent across the wire here so this should only ever be done locally between a wallet client and a local agent. This is not secure to do over the internet.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
curl -s -X PUT "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/boot" \
  -H 'accept: */*' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data "{\"name\": \"${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE}\",
           \"salt\": \"${EXPLORER_SALT}\"}" \
  | jq '.["msg"]' | tr -d '"'

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Keystore and Inception Review
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once you get the hang of creating keystores and performing inception events then you will see the similarities between the KLI and the Agent API. When you have finished creating all of the keystores you need, one each for the Explorer, Librarian, Wise Man, and Gatekeeper, then you are ready to move on to the next step of connecting all of the agents together using out of band introductions, or OOBIs.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Out of Band Introductions (OOBIs) Between Controllers
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Routes in a routing table or DNS entries are internet discovery system analogues to help understand the purpose out of band introductions. ATHENA requires a number of components in the Abydos network to talk to each other and thus an OOBI must be created or “resolved” for each connection between components.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Out of Band Introductions (OOBIs) Between Controllers

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: riable. Keystore and Inception Review Once you get the hang of creating keystores and performing inception events then you will see the similarities between the KLI and the Agent API. When you have finished creating all of the keystores you need, one each for the Explorer, Librarian, Wise Man, and Gatekeeper, then you are ready to move on to the next step of connecting all of the agents together using out of band introductions, or OOBIs. Out of Band Introductions (OOBIs) Between Controllers Routes in a routing table or DNS entries are internet discovery system analogues to help understand the purpose out of band introductions. ATHENA requires a number of components in the Abydos network to talk to each other and thus an OOBI must be created or “resolved” for each connection between components.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Out of Band Introductions (OOBIs) Between Controllers

author: Kent Bull
content: You can think of an OOBI as an address with a role for one controller to contact either another controller or a KERI resource such as a credential schema. In order for one controller to be able to issue a credential to another controller it must know where that other controller is. The role of an OOBI determines what to use the OOBI for, such as a witness, a credential schema, or just a contact.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Out of Band Introductions (OOBIs) Between Controllers

author: Kent Bull
content: For example, in the diagram above, in order for Ramiel to issue the TreasureHuntingJourney credential to Richard he must have an OOBI that shows the location of Richard that includes a witness for Richard. This witness OOBI is necessary so Ramiel can retrieve and verify Richard’s KEL from the witness and ensure it matches up to the AID prefix included in the OOBI URL. The “wan” witness is used in this case.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Out of Band Introductions (OOBIs) Between Controllers

author: Kent Bull
content: OOBIs described
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Out of band introductions (OOBIs) are the discovery mechanism for KERI controllers and KERI objects such as ACDC Schemas or cached ACDCs. OOBIs tell one controller how to reach another controller or object and are identified both by an AID prefix as well as an alias label.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A witness OOBI looks like the following example:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
"http://127.0.0.1:5642/oobi/EJS0-vv_OPAQCdJLmkd5dT0EW-mOfhn_Cje4yzRjTv8q/witness/BBilc4-L3tFUnfM_wJr4S4OJanAv_VmF_dJNN6vkf2Ha"

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: "http://127.0.0.1:5642/oobi/EJS0-vv_OPAQCdJLmkd5dT0EW-mOfhn_Cje4yzRjTv8q/witness/BBilc4-L3tFUnfM_wJr4S4OJanAv_VmF_dJNN6vkf2Ha"
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As you can see there are two KERI AID prefixes, one for the controller and another for the witness. The template for this OOBI URL is as follows:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
[proto]://[wit host]:[wit port]/oobi/[controller prefix]/[role]/[wit prefix]

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: [proto]://[wit host]:[wit port]/oobi/[controller prefix]/[role]/[wit prefix]
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The terminology “out-of-band” here refers to the fact that the general purpose internet infrastructure like IP routers, gateways, and DNS servers, are not part of KERI and are thus out-of-band with respect to KERI. The primary advantage to using this general purpose infrastructure for service discovery means KERI doesn’t have to provide it’s own discovery mechanism since it can piggy back on the existing internet infrastructure.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Discovery over the internet includes endpoint discovery of witnesses, watchers, and other network participants including jurors and judges (not yet implemented). According to the Out-Of-Band-Introduction (OOBI) Protocol specification:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In the specification the usage of the terms “endpoint” or “service endpoint” just mean the URL to use to access some KERI resource.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: OOBIs must be verified
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The above quote draws attention to the fact that that OOBI URLs themselves are not trustable and must be verified. What does this mean? What is being verified? Since OOBI URLs merely point to a controller’s AID (prefix) the OOBI must be used to retrieve the full key event log from that controller referenced by the prefix. This key event log is then verified using the witness identified by the witness prefix at the end of the OOBI, in the case of a witness OOBI. If the controller proves control over the key referenced by the KEL then the verifier of the OOBI can then trust they have securely discovered the controller referenced by the OOBI.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: OOBIs may have roles
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: This is an OOBI that shows a witness for a given prefix. This type of OOBI includes a “witness” role and a witness prefix. This witness prefix corresponds to the witness host and port earlier in the OOBI URL. This witness will have a full key event log (KEL) of all of that controller’s key events and thus can be used to verify that the AID prefix of the controller’s KEL verifies against the controller prefix included in the OOBI URL. This verification process is a part of what is known as “resolving” an OOBI.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: See kering.py:L23 for a full list of other OOBI roles including controller, witness, registrar, watcher, judge, juror, peer, mailbox, and agent, at present.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Richard to Ramiel OOBIs – pairwise exchange

author: Kent Bull
content: Since both witness OOBIs use the “wan” witness to resolve key state they have a secondary arrow pointing to wan in addition to their primary arrow to indicate this relationship.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Richard to Ramiel OOBIs – pairwise exchange

author: Kent Bull
content: Each controller wanting to communicate with another controller must resolve an OOBI with the target controller describing where the controller is located over the internet because otherwise the two controllers would not know where the other is. Resolving OOBIs in a pairwise manner enables the source controller to send messages to that destination controller.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Richard to Ramiel OOBIs – pairwise exchange

author: Kent Bull
content: In the case of Ramiel an OOBI must be resolved for both Elayne and Richard in order for Ramiel to issue the TreasureHuntingJourney credential to them because Ramiel must know where Elayne and Richard are over the internet to be able to communicate with them. Similarly, Elayne and Richard both must resolve an OOBI that describes where Ramiel is located over the internet. All of these introductions are performed in both the make_introductions_kli and make_introductions_agent functions in the workflow.sh script.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Richard to Ramiel OOBIs – pairwise exchange

author: Kent Bull
content: Abydos OOBIs
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The journey to Abydos requires that each controller is known for a certain reason. These reasons may include:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credential Issuance: an issuing controller must have an OOBI for each issuee’s controller receiving a credential. This is the case when Ramiel needs to issue the TreasureHuntingJourney credential to both Richard and Elayne. Likewise, Richard must know how to contact Ramiel in order to issue him the JourneyMarkRequest credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credential Presentation: a credential holding controller must have the OOBI for the target node they will be sending a credential presentation to so that the presentation can reach the intended destination. This is the case when Richard and Elayne want to send the JourneyCharter credential to Zaqiel in order to be permitted entrance into Abydos.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: These OOBI introductions are the controller-to-controller OOBIs needed during network operation. Keep in mind that there were OOBIs resolved in network setup and configuration for each of the witnesses as well as the four credential schemas used.

BASH This should result in a 202 HTTP status code when successful. Once you have performed all OOBI creations for the controllers as depicted in the diagram above then move on to creating credential registries. Credential Registries
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In order to issue credentials from one controller to the next a credential registry must exist in both the issuing controller’s keystore to create and issue the credential from as well as in the receiving controller’s (issuee’s) keystore to receive the issued credential. This requires adding a new concept to manage the credential registry as well as any credentials issued from the registry. Enter the transaction event log transaction event log (TEL).
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Transaction Event Logs (TELs)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A transaction event log is a log of all events occurring for a verifiable credential or a credential registry that manages verifiable credentials. All TEL events are anchored in a KEL in either ixn (interaction) or rot (rotation) events. This is the foundation enabling a verifiable credential protocol to be built on top of KERI. This protocol is known as the authentic chained data container, or ACDC protocol. See the ACDC specification for a complete description of this protocol.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ixn
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: rot
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Two further sub-protocols within the ACDC paradigm further elaborate on creating registries and handling the verifiable credential lifecycle. These are the Public Transaction Event Logs (PTEL) and the Issuance and Presentation Exchange (IPEX) protocols.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Public Transaction Event Logs (PTEL) protocol
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: PTEL has two major parts the first being the details of using transaction event logs (TELs) to connect ACDCs to key event logs (KELs) and the second being the handling of events in the lifecycle of a credential including issuance and revocation.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: There are two types of TELs:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A management TEL for managing an ACDC (credential) registry and tracks the list of Registrars that will act as Backers for individual TELs for each verifiable credential (VC).
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A Verifiable Credential TEL (VC TEL) for managing the issued and revoked state of an individual ACDC. The VC TEL also contains a reference to its corresponding management TEL. There are only two events in a VC TEL which are the issuance event and then a subsequent revocation event, if ever revoked.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull

author: Kent Bull

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Building upon the PTEL specification IPEX describes what happens between credential registries. This protocol defines the disclosure workflow events used to share information between the Discloser and the Disclosee.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

Registry Creation  Image A registry creation event occurs in a TEL and has the type vcp. See the “Verifiable Credential Registry” section of the PTEL specification for a complete description of TEL event types. To avoid overburdening the diagram below the individual ixn events for each credential issuance are not included in the KEL nor are the iss events included in the TELs. The blue credential graphic to the right is used on successive versions of the diagram to represent credential issuances. author: Kent Bull
content: There is no TOAD (threshold of accountable duplicity) argument for the KLI since as of March 2023 it has not been needed nor has the external backer feature been used or tested in production. Nonetheless a default TOAD is computed based on the length of the backer list sent in as you can see on Line 95 of eventing.py. If you need this feature then be sure to open an issue on the KERIpy GitHub repo and attend a community dev meeting (every other Tuesday at 8 AM UTC-6 DST / UTC-7 Non-DST).
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Creating a registry using the Agent API is similar to the KLI except that the values passed as arguments to kli vc registry incept are instead located in the body as a JSON object.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli vc registry incept
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
curl -s -X POST "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/registries" \
  -H "accept: */*" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"alias\":\"${EXPLORER_ALIAS}\",
       \"baks\": [],
       \"estOnly\":false,
       \"name\":\"${EXPLORER_REGISTRY}\",
       \"noBackers\":true,
       \"toad\":0}" \
  | jq

BASH Once again here the output is piped to “jq” though this is only to make the response easy on the eyes. Next we move to one of the most exciting parts of this journey, the issuing of credentials! Credential Issuance  Image Value generation from the entire decentralized identity space centers around verifiable credentials. Wrap some data attributes in a container defined by a schema and you now have a verifiable bearer instrument that is a part of your digital reputation. A credential is the fundamental unit, or currency, of reputation. Name – Why ACDC? Now where does the name authentic chained data container come from and how did it get attached to the verifiable credential space? Three points make this clear: Since ACDC-style credentials are verifiable as authentic using the KERI decentralized key management infrastructure (DKMI),
since ACDC-style credentials can be easily chained as a directed, acyclic graph (DAG),
and since the schema of an ACDC creates a shape or container for data,
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Purpose
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The introductory quote from the ACDC spec sums up well the purpose of the ACDC protocol:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Verifiable authorizations, permissions, rights, and credentials that include a full provenance chain anchored in key event logs make KERI an elegant way to capture and unlock reputation value as well as serve as a robust solution for identity and access management systems.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To learn in-depth about all the particulars for ACDCs read the Authentic Chained Data Containers (ACDC) IETF draft specification by Dr. Samuel M. Smith.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: With this brief interlude on the name and purpose of ACDCs out of the way next we dive into the specific credentials we will issue for the journey to Abydos.

With this brief interlude on the name and purpose of ACDCs out of the way next we dive into the specific credentials we will issue for the journey to Abydos. Credentials for Abydos Multiple different credentials are issued during different parts of the overall ATHENA trust workflow in the journey to Abydos. The following graphic shows a small blue icon representing each issued (OUT) or received (IN) credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The four credentials that will be issued over the course of the journey include:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: TreasureHuntingJourney: this is issued by the Wise Man to each of the explorers, Richard and Elayne, to tell them about a potential journey they could go on. It represents a commitment by ATHENA to a potential explorer as an offer of a journey they could embark upon.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: JourneyMarkRequest: this is issued by an explorer to the Wise Man as a commitment to join the party for a particular TreasureHuntingJourney. Both Richard and Elayne issue this to Ramiel.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: JourneyMark: this is issued by the Wise Man to an explorer as an acknowledgment that ATHENA accepted that explorer onto the requested TreasureHuntingJourney. This is issued by Ramiel to both Richard and Elayne.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: JourneyCharter: once the party threshold for a journey is reached (threshold logic not yet implemented) the Wise Man issues a JourneyCharter credential to each party member who will be going on the journey. This includes Richard and Elayne.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: On to our first credential!
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Issue TreasureHuntingJourney Credential – KLI
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The TreasureHuntingJourney must be issued to both Richard and Elayne as depicted in the graphic below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta:
equest: this is issued by an explorer to the Wise Man as a commitment to join the party for a particular TreasureHuntingJourney. Both Richard and Elayne issue this to Ramiel.
JourneyMark: this is issued by the Wise Man to an explorer as an acknowledgment that ATHENA accepted that explorer onto the requested TreasureHuntingJourney. This is issued by Ramiel to both Richard and Elayne.
JourneyCharter: once the party threshold for a journey is reached (threshold logic not yet implemented) the Wise Man issues a JourneyCharter credential to each party member who will be going on the journey. This includes Richard and Elayne. On to our first credential! Issue TreasureHuntingJourney Credential – KLI The TreasureHuntingJourney must be issued to both Richard and Elayne as depicted in the graphic below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As you see Ramiel has both of these credentials in the OUT column to indicate he has issued these credentials and both Richard and Elayne have them in the IN column indicating they have received these credentials.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The following command shows issuance of this credential to Richard.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli vc issue --name ${WISEMAN_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${WISEMAN_ALIAS} \
  --registry-name ${WISEMAN_REGISTRY} \
  --schema "${TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID}" \
  --recipient "${RICHARD_PREFIX}" \
  --data @"${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_data/osireion-treasure-hunting-journey.json

author: Kent Bull
content: Once the credential is issued using kli vc issue it can take a few moments to arrive so the kli vc list ... --poll command is useful to wait for the credential to arrive. Make sure to list the credentials because polling causes internal KERI messages to be delivered.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli vc issue
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli vc list ... --poll
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

kli vc list --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} --poll

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Issuing credentials with the Agent is very similar to the command line except all of the data arguments are added as JSON body attributes as shown below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Issue TreasureHuntingJourney Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content:
JOURNEY_DATA=$(cat "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_data/osireion-treasure-hunting-journey.json)
curl -s -X POST "${WISEMAN_AGENT_URL}/credentials/${WISEMAN_ALIAS}" \
  -H "accept: application/json" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"credentialData\":${JOURNEY_DATA},
       \"recipient\":\"${RICHARD_PREFIX}\",
       \"registry\":\"${WISEMAN_REGISTRY}\",
       \"schema\":\"${TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID}\"}" \
  | jq '.["d"]' | tr -d '"'

author: Kent Bull
content: Advanced Credential Issuance – with Edges and Rules
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: ACDCs with Edges
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The preceding credential issuance of TreasureHuntingJourney credentials was interesting yet it did not include one of the critical features of ACDCs: edges. Since a graph of ACDCs is connected by edges then knowing how to use an edge to connect, or “chain,” credentials together enables you to represent your domain’s credential data as a graph data structure, specifically a directed acyclic graph, which is just an append-only data structure. The JourneyMarkRequest credential includes an edge that points to the TreasureHuntingJourney credential as shown in the graph below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
imgMeta: e combination to selects the “d” attribute from the first credential in the list (“.[0]”) returned and stores it in the EXPLORER_JOURNEY_CRED_SAID variable. Advanced Credential Issuance – with Edges and Rules ACDCs with Edges The preceding credential issuance of TreasureHuntingJourney credentials was interesting yet it did not include one of the critical features of ACDCs: edges. Since a graph of ACDCs is connected by edges then knowing how to use an edge to connect, or “chain,” credentials together enables you to represent your domain’s credential data as a graph data structure, specifically a directed acyclic graph, which is just an append-only data structure. The JourneyMarkRequest credential includes an edge that points to the TreasureHuntingJourney credential as shown in the graph below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The edge from the JourneyMarkRequest is labeled as “journey”. This is represented in the schema for the JourneyMarkRequest as well as in the schema map JSON file that KASLCred reads in. Edges can be traversed to resolve, inspect, and validate the credential the edge points to.

content: A few steps are involved in issuing the JourneyMarkRequest credential due to the fact that it points to another credential, the TreasureHuntingJourney credential. These steps include:

All of these steps are used to write a JSON file with the edge data that will be included to either the --data argument to kli vc issue with the KLI or embedded within the “source” attribute of the JSON object POSTed to "/credentials/{alias}” with the Agent.

author: Kent Bull
content: JQ filters
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: JQ filters are useful to write values to a JQ object which eventually will become JSON. See the “Object Construction” section of the “Types and Values” section of the JQ documentation for a full explanation. In the below code, as well as in the issue_treasurehuntingjourney_credentials_agent function in workflow.sh the following JQ object is used to construct an edge:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: workflow.sh
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The BASH variable “TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID” is added in before the echo statement writes that JQ object to the file “JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER”. This SAID is what it seems, the SAID of the TreasureHuntingJourney ACDC Schema. This is written into the JQ filter object.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: echo
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: JQ is then used to add the “EXPLORER_JOURNEY_SAID” into the “n” field of the JQ filter using the period “.” expression with the input piped into jq to output the resulting edge JSON.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: jq
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER=${ATHENA_DIR}/credential_edges/richard-journey-edge-filter.jq
RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE=${ATHENA_DIR}/credential_edges/richard-journey-edge.json
echo "{d: \"\", journey: {n: ., s: \"${TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID}\"}}" >"${JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER}"
EXPLORER_JOURNEY_SAID=$(kli vc list --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said \
  --schema "${TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID}")
echo \""${EXPLORER_JOURNEY_SAID}"\" \
  | jq -f "${JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER}" >"${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE}"
kli saidify --file "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE}"
# Add SAID to the JourneyMarkRequest rules
kli saidify --file "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_rules/journey-mark-request-rules.json

author: Kent Bull
content: SAIDify TreasureHuntingJourneyEdge
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Finally after writing the edge JSON the file is SAIDified with kli saidify. This populates the “d” attribute of the JSON with a self addressing identifier, or SAID. A self addressing identifier, according to the spec, is:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli saidify
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: This essentially means the value of the “d” field is computed from the value of everything else in the JSON object and as such is a value derived from everything else in the JSON object, thus self-addressing. Read the spec to learn about the specialized derivation function required to verify a SAID.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Performing the issuance of the JourneyMarkRequest with the Mark I Agent is very similar to using. the KLI. The primary difference is that the JSON files passed in to --data, --edges, and --rules are placed inside the JSON body of the HTTP POST request to the /credentials/{alias}/ route.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Prepare TreasureHuntingJourney Edge
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The preparation steps here are pretty much identical to preparing the TreasureHuntingJourney edge for the KLI version of this section.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
# Add SAID to JourneyMarkRequest rules
kli saidify --file "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_rules/journey-mark-request-rules.json
RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER=${ATHENA_DIR}/credential_edges/richard-journey-edge-filter.jq
RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE=${ATHENA_DIR}/credential_edges/richard-journey-edge.json
echo "{d: \"\", journey: {n: ., s: \"${TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID}\"}}" >"${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER}"
EXPLORER_JOURNEY_SAID=$(curl -s -X GET "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/credentials/${EXPLORER_ALIAS}?type=received&schema=${TREASURE_HUNTING_JOURNEY_SCHEMA_SAID}" | jq '.[0] | .sad.d' | tr -d '"')
echo \""${EXPLORER_JOURNEY_SAID}"\" | jq -f "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE_FILTER}" >"${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE}"
kli saidify --file "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE}"

content: Issue JourneyMarkRequest Credential
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: To simplify issuing this credential from the workflow.sh script the contents of the JSON files for the data, rules, and edges are stored in BASH variables using the **VARNAME=$(cat FILENAME)** pattern. This makes it easy to include the contents in the curl command below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: workflow.sh
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: **VARNAME=$(cat FILENAME)**
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: curl
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
REQUEST_RULES=$(cat "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_rules/journey-mark-request-rules.json)
RICHARD_MARK_DATA=$(cat "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_data/journey-mark-request-data-richard.json)
RICHARD_EDGE_DATA=$(cat "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE}")
curl -s -X POST "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/credentials/${EXPLORER_ALIAS}" \
  -H "accept: application/json" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"credentialData\":${RICHARD_MARK_DATA},
       \"recipient\":\"${WISEMAN_PREFIX}\",
       \"registry\":\"${EXPLORER_REGISTRY}\",
       \"schema\":\"${JOURNEY_MARK_REQUEST_SCHEMA_SAID}\",
       \"source\":${RICHARD_EDGE_DATA},
       \"rules\":${REQUEST_RULES}}" \
  | jq '.d' | tr -d '"'
# since there is no polling then sleep to wait for credential delivery
sleep 5

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
REQUEST_RULES=$(cat "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_rules/journey-mark-request-rules.json)
RICHARD_MARK_DATA=$(cat "${ATHENA_DIR}"/credential_data/journey-mark-request-data-richard.json)
RICHARD_EDGE_DATA=$(cat "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_EDGE}")
curl -s -X POST "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/credentials/${EXPLORER_ALIAS}" \
  -H "accept: application/json" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"credentialData\":${RICHARD_MARK_DATA},
       \"recipient\":\"${WISEMAN_PREFIX}\",
       \"registry\":\"${EXPLORER_REGISTRY}\",
       \"schema\":\"${JOURNEY_MARK_REQUEST_SCHEMA_SAID}\",
       \"source\":${RICHARD_EDGE_DATA},
       \"rules\":${REQUEST_RULES}}" \
  | jq '.d' | tr -d '"'
# since there is no polling then sleep to wait for credential delivery
sleep 5
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: There doesn’t exist an equivalent to kli vc list ... --poll in the KERIpy Mark I agent so you can perform a sleep 5 or something similar for an equivalent wait to ensure that a request to **/credentials/{alias}/type=issued&schema={schema_said}** will return the issued credential. You could also manually poll this endpoint, process the JSON array of credentials returned, and then continue to poll if the return body doesn’t contain the expected credential.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credential Presentation
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In order for a decentralized identity credentialing system to have any value it must be able to plug into a business process somewhere. Credential presentation is the beginning of plugging in a credentialing system to your custom business process logic. Your primary option here is to write a custom controller. The “Sally” project is one such custom controller. It treats KERIpy as a library on top of which to layer in your custom business logic. I did precisely this in my fork of Sally (a.k.a. Abydos Gatekeeper), which I explain below.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Custom business logic must plug directly in to the credential processing workflow within a controller and respond to credential validation actions that occur when a credential is presented based on whether the credential is issued or revoked. The point at which the custom business logic comes into contact with the decentralized identity system are in custom handler functions written in your custom controller.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A webhook is called from the custom handler functions to illustrate how you would signal controller events to an app, possibly a wallet app, waiting to be notified of event completions from the KERI Agent.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: This is the most complicated, least explained portion of this tutorial. If you want to stay out of the inner details of the custom controller and out of KERIpy then just follow the commands listed in the workflow.sh script. On the other hand, if you are willing to venture into this depth I will assume you have some familiarity with Python as well as the bravery to confront the innards of the flow-based programming, hierarchical structured concurrency asynchronous runtime library Dr. Smith wrote, ioflo/hio, that is at the core of both KERIpy and the Sally custom controller.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: After reading through the controller code, or KERIpy code, you may find yourself wanting to write a custom controller either with a different async framework or another language, which are reasonable desires. I encourage you to dig a little deeper into the HIO code and the KERIpy code before rewriting it. I expect to have a future blog post on the HIO library as well as its usage in KERIpy, though do what. you think is most reasonable. I offer this suggestion just to encourage you to become familiar with the core KERI internals mostly so we can increase the core contributor count to KERIpy. Many hands make light work.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Communicator class
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The Communicator class in sally/src/sally/core/handling.py is our main focus. The primary difference between the GLEIF-IT upstream repository and my fork of Sally is that I made a dict of schema handlers keyed by the names of credentials so it would be easy to swap in new schema SAIDs to be recognized as I wrote the schema files during initial development of the schemas. I also thought it readable and human-friendly to use schema type names like “TreasureHuntingJourney” as dict keys rather than schema prefixes like “EIxAox3KEhiQ_yCwXWeriQ3ruPWbgK94NDDkHAZCuP9l”.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Technically you could hardcode the schema prefixes into your code since once you standardize on a specific set of ACDC schemas and they are in production then you won’t be changing them especially since you must support backwards compatibility likely indefinitely for whatever schemas you release to production. Yet, even with this technical possibility, I prefer to use schema type names so I wrote a a resolver function to resolve a schema.

BASH Technically you could hardcode the schema prefixes into your code since once you standardize on a specific set of ACDC schemas and they are in production then you won’t be changing them especially since you must support backwards compatibility likely indefinitely for whatever schemas you release to production. Yet, even with this technical possibility, I prefer to use schema type names so I wrote a a resolver function to resolve a schema. Communicator init function You can see in the init function I made a dict for schema handlers and another dict for payload handlers that maps the type names above to specific functions.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: I then use two resolver functions, type to SAID and SAID to type, to pair up schema SAIDs from incoming presentations to the configured SAIDs from the schema SAID mapping file we wrote earlier:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: If no mapping is found for a given type then a ValidationError is raised.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Schema Validator Function Selection and Invocation
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: After resolving the schema type for an incoming credential then a handler is selected from the configured SAID mapping file as shown on line 185 in order to select and then invoke a credential validator:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Schema Validator Function Selection and Invocation

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Schema Validator Function Selection and Invocation

author: Kent Bull
content: Payload Handler Function Selection and Invocation
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: After selecting the validator, and assuming the credential passes validation, a response payload handler is selected on line 246 and then invoked.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Payload Handler Function Selection and Invocation

author: Kent Bull
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Payload Handler Function Selection and Invocation

author: Kent Bull
content: Again, if no schema validator or handler is found an exception is raised. Otherwise the correct handler is selected to validate the schema and then the correct payload handler prepares the response to the presentation event.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Payload Handler Function Selection and Invocation

author: Kent Bull
content: Both of the following functions are the most complicated versions of their functions in Abydos Gatekeeper. You can read through the other similar functions if you want more illustrations.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Payload Handler Function Selection and Invocation

author: Kent Bull
content: JourneyCharter schema validator
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: As you can see in the validateJourneyCharter function first the ACDC schema SAID is validated, the credential issuer is verified, and then the credential is handed to other validation functions to validate the full credential chain. Refer to the code for a complete understanding of what it means to validate the full credential chain.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: In response to a credential presentation the custom controller can return a customized object which. in this case is the data dict beginning at line 511. What makes this payload function more interesting is the use of the registry (reger) object to retrieve data from each of the credentials chained to the JourneyCharter credential.

author: Kent Bull
content: With all of this setup complete we are ready to set up the webhook.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Webhook Setup
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The webhook only starts up a HTTP server on the default port of 9923. You start it up with the following:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
$ sally hook demo
# Should provide output like the following:
launching on port 9923
Sally Web Hook Sample listening on 9923

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
$ sally hook demo
# Should provide output like the following:
launching on port 9923
Sally Web Hook Sample listening on 9923
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once the webhook is listening then you are ready to make a presentation and receive a response from the controller.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Present JourneyCharter Credential – KLI
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Making a presentation request requires knowing the SAID of the credential you want to present as well as the alias of the recipient you want to send the presentation to. This alias must match up the OOBI alias name you used earlier when resolving an OOBI to connect the presenting controller to the target controller the presentation is being sent to.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
# First we get the SAID of the JourneyCharter credential for Richard
RICHARD_CRED_SAID=$(kli vc list --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said \
  --schema ${JOURNEY_CHARTER_SCHEMA_SAID})
# Then we present the credential to the Gatekeeper
kli vc present --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said "${RICHARD_CRED_SAID}" \
  --recipient ${GATEKEEPER_ALIAS} \
  --include

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
# First we get the SAID of the JourneyCharter credential for Richard
RICHARD_CRED_SAID=$(kli vc list --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said \
  --schema ${JOURNEY_CHARTER_SCHEMA_SAID})
# Then we present the credential to the Gatekeeper
kli vc present --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said "${RICHARD_CRED_SAID}" \
  --recipient ${GATEKEEPER_ALIAS} \
  --include
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The --include option instructs the KLI to send all of the ACDC data in the presentation and is critical in order for the request to work.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --include
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once the presentation is sent you will need to wait a few moments for the target controller to receive the credential, validate it, and then send the response payload to the webhook.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Webhook Response
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Present JourneyCharter Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Presenting with the agent is very similar to with the KLI. You must know the recipient alias, corresponding to the “oobialias” property used to register an OOBI earlier, you must know the ACDC schema SAID, as well as the SAID of the credential you want to present.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Present JourneyCharter Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content:
EXPLORER_CHARTER_CRED_SAID=$(curl -s \
  -X GET "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/credentials/${EXPLORER_ALIAS}?type=received&schema=${JOURNEY_CHARTER_SCHEMA_SAID}" \
  | jq '.[0] | .sad.d' | tr -d '"')
curl -s \
  -X POST "${EXPLORER_AGENT_URL}/credentials/${EXPLORER_ALIAS}/presentations" \
  -H 'accept: */*' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{
        \"recipient\": \"${GATEKEEPER_ALIAS}\",
        \"said\": \"${EXPLORER_CHARTER_CRED_SAID}\",
        \"schema\": \"${JOURNEY_CHARTER_SCHEMA_SAID}\",
        \"include\": true
      }"

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Present JourneyCharter Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)

  | jq '.[0] | .sad.d' | tr -d '"')
curl -s \

        \"include\": true
      }"
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Once again the **"include": true** property is essential to instruct the agent to send all of the ACDC data for the JourneyMark credential and all linked credentials to the recipient controller, the Abydos Gatekeeper.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Present JourneyCharter Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content: **"include": true**
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Credential Revocation
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Revocation is an essential part of the lifecycle of an ACDC-style credential. A credential may be revoked at any time. This is represented as a “rev” action in the TEL for that ACDC and can be included in either an “ixn” (Interaction) or “rot” (Rotation) event in the controller’s KEL.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Revoke JourneyCharter Credential – KLI
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Revoking a credential is very simple. Starting with the SAID of the credential to be revoked the below example stores it in the BASH variable RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID which is then fed into the kli vc revoke command.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli vc revoke
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID=$(kli vc list \
  --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said \
  --schema ${JOURNEY_CHARTER_SCHEMA_SAID})
kli vc revoke --name ${WISEMAN_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${WISEMAN_ALIAS} \
  --registry-name ${WISEMAN_REGISTRY} \
  --said "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID}" \
  --send ${GATEKEEPER_ALIAS}

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID=$(kli vc list \
  --name ${EXPLORER_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${EXPLORER_ALIAS} \
  --said \
  --schema ${JOURNEY_CHARTER_SCHEMA_SAID})
kli vc revoke --name ${WISEMAN_KEYSTORE} \
  --alias ${WISEMAN_ALIAS} \
  --registry-name ${WISEMAN_REGISTRY} \
  --said "${RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID}" \
  --send ${GATEKEEPER_ALIAS}
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Then when this credential is presented again later the Gatekeeper will see that the credential has been revoked.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The --send argument is essential to instruct the issuing controller to send the revocation notice to the target controller, in this case the Gatekeeper.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: --send
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Webhook revocation notification
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: With the revocation information the Gatekeeper adjusts its internal state for that credential and then triggers the webhook with a revocation payload. The output from the webhook looks like the following:

}

author: Kent Bull
content: Presenting a Revoked Credential
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: When you try to present the credential again to the Gatekeeper the revocation status is detected.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli vc present --name explorer --alias richard \
  --said ${RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID} \
  --recipient zaqiel \
  --include

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
kli vc present --name explorer --alias richard \
  --said ${RICHARD_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID} \
  --recipient zaqiel \
  --include
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Yet the Gatekeeper detects the revoked status:
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
revoked credential EIdNBtudTLx3g-WWIYmuvzkUmVnXgT3EdaUC-3JbeAv1 being presented
# ...continues on to send revoked credential to the webhook

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
revoked credential EIdNBtudTLx3g-WWIYmuvzkUmVnXgT3EdaUC-3JbeAv1 being presented
# ...continues on to send revoked credential to the webhook
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: The one thing missing from the Abydos Gatekeeper for this revocation flow is to send a different payload to the webhook when a revoked credential is presented. This topic will be explored in a future blog post.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: With that complete we move on to the bonus section on revocation with the Mark I agent.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: Bonus – Revoke JourneyCharter Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: A simple revocation is possible with the Mark I Agent yet it is missing functionality similar to the --send argument to kli vc revoke and thus should not be used. It is included here as an illustration of how revocation with an agent may work.
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Bonus – Revoke JourneyCharter Credential – Agent (Mark I – deprecated)

author: Kent Bull
content: --send
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content: kli vc revoke
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

author: Kent Bull
content:
curl -s \
  -X DELETE "${WISEMAN_AGENT}/credentials/${WISEMAN_ALIAS}?registry=${WISEMAN_REGISTRY}&said=${EXPLORER_JOURNEY_CHARTER_CRED_SAID}" \
  --header 'accept: */*' \
  --header 'Content-Type: application/json'

BASH
pageTitle: KERI Tutorial Series: Treasure Hunting in Abydos! Issuing and Verifying a Credential (ACDC)

  --header 'Content-Type: application/json'
